Finland is an extremely low-conflict country in ACLED terms, and the question likely requires roughly 11 or more ACLED-coded fatalities in a 30-day window because the known reference value on 2025-10-30 was 1.000. That implies essentially no recent baseline fatalities. Reaching the threshold would probably require a rare, exceptional terrorist/extremist mass-casualty event or an unusual cluster of politically motivated killings/riots. Evidence available by 2025-11-09 points to an elevated but still modest threat environment: Finnish security services kept the terror threat at level 2/4 and noted growing far-right risk, but the main concern remained lone actors or small groups rather than sustained campaigns. Recent 2025 violent incidents found in search were low-casualty and reportedly non-political, which makes them poor candidates for ACLED fatalities. Finland has had disrupted far-right plots in recent years, so the probability is not zero, but absent evidence of a concrete imminent plot or any late-2025 fatal ACLED-relevant incident, the chance of exceeding 10x baseline in any given 30-day window remains low. I assign the nearest date the floor-like probability because most of that window had already elapsed with no sign of a qualifying event, then edge probabilities upward for later dates to reflect greater uncertainty with longer forecast horizons.

The key anchor is the prompt’s statement that the latest known 360-day average of ACLED 'Violence against civilians' in Saint Vincent and the Grenadines was 0.000 as of 2025-10-30. That strongly suggests zero ACLED-coded VAC events in roughly the preceding year, so the monthly hazard is low. Because the reference average is effectively zero, resolution likely boils down to whether there is at least one ACLED-coded VAC event in each 30-day window; a large backfill high enough to require 2+ events seems unlikely.

I do see meaningful upside risk from the country’s broader violence environment: multiple sources describe elevated gang/gun violence and very high homicide totals in 2024-2025, and local reporting shows violent deaths continuing into October 2025. But the same evidence also shows an important distinction: SVG can have many homicides without ACLED recording any as 'Violence against civilians.' That implies most killings are being treated as ordinary crime, interpersonal violence, or otherwise not meeting ACLED’s organized-armed-group-on-civilians standard.

For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, and I found October homicide reports but no clear evidence of a qualifying VAC event in late October/early November, so I keep this lowest. For 2025-12-09 and especially 2026-02-07, the probabilities rise modestly toward 0.5 because more of the window lies in the future and there is more time for a gang-linked or otherwise ACLED-qualifying attack to occur, but the structural base rate remains low.

Threshold is effectively about 11+ ACLED battle events in a 30-day window, because the known reference value is 1.000 as of late October and likely remains near that. Lithuania had no evident battle trend going into the forecast date; recent incidents were worrying but mostly hybrid/non-kinetic: airspace violations, drones, and smuggling balloons rather than sustained armed clashes. Against that, Baltic officials and a German general were warning that Russia could mount limited action against NATO, and Lithuania was clearly on edge. 

For 2025-11-16, the window is mostly already known by the forecast date, so getting to 11+ battles would require a sudden major escalation within about a week; that pushes me to the floor. For 2025-12-09, there is a full future 30-day window, so odds are a bit higher but still low because NATO deterrence and Russia's existing commitments make a Lithuanian kinetic outbreak unlikely. For 2026-02-07, there is more time for a crisis to emerge, so the probability rises modestly and is closer to 0.5 as required, but it remains well below even odds because this still requires an actual organized armed clash inside Lithuania severe enough to generate 11+ ACLED battle events.

French Polynesia appears to be a very low-base-rate case for ACLED 'Battles.' The prompt’s latest known reference value was 1.000 as of 2025-10-30, which strongly suggests essentially zero battles in the prior 360 days and implies a threshold of about 11+ battle events in a 30-day window for a YES resolution. My searches found no direct evidence of militant separatism, armed-group clashes, or a security emergency in French Polynesia itself. The main recent mobilization there was a large anti-meth protest movement, which was reported as peaceful protest activity rather than armed violence. The pro-independence movement was described as gradualist and institutional, not militant. The main tail risks are (1) regional precedent from New Caledonia’s sudden violent deterioration, and (2) significant drug-trafficking activity that could, in theory, generate organized armed confrontations or unusual ACLED coding. Even so, going from an apparent zero-battle baseline to 11+ battles in any of these 30-day windows remains very unlikely. I therefore keep the near-term dates at or near the floor, with a modestly higher probability by February because there is more time for an unexpected security deterioration.

Baseline is effectively zero, so this mostly reduces to: will ACLED record at least one riot in Myanmar in each target 30-day window? Recent evidence points to a very low riot base rate even amid extreme violence: Myanmar’s 2025 conflict is described mainly in terms of armed clashes and air/drone strikes, not crowd violence (search_0_result_2), and the prompt says the recent 360-day riot average was 0.000. That suggests riot-coded events have been absent or nearly absent. Additional downward pressure comes from the junta’s July 2025 law banning election criticism/protests with severe penalties, which should deter visible street mobilization (search_1_result_2), and Reuters/others describing the anti-junta movement as having shifted from mass protest toward armed rebellion rather than street action (search_2_result_7). The main upside risk is the planned Dec 2025/Jan 2026 election: multiple sources warn it could increase resistance, protest, and violence (search_1_result_2, search_2_result_5, search_2_result_6). That makes the February window materially riskier than the November and early-December windows. The 2025-11-16 probability is especially low because most of that 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, leaving only about a week for a first riot to occur.

The reference value is about 1.0 as of the latest known update, so the practical threshold is roughly 11 ACLED-coded 'Violence against civilians' events in a 30-day window. For Albania, that is a very high bar. The evidence available before 2025-11-09 points to a low-conflict environment with sporadic organized-crime and targeted attacks, not an active insurgency or sustained wave of civilian targeting. The October 2025 courtroom killing shows non-zero tail risk, and gangland violence is a real background factor, but I found no sign of a nationwide escalation that would make 11+ VAC events in a month likely. For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, which further suppresses the chance of a sudden jump to the threshold. I raise probabilities slightly for later dates because more lead time means more opportunity for an unforeseen criminal or political violence cluster to emerge, but the event remains unlikely overall.

The effective threshold is about 11+ ACLED-coded battle events in a 30-day window, because the known reference value near the cutoff was 1.000. Jordan is a low-battle country, and 2025 reporting points to isolated border clashes rather than a sustained battle tempo. Reuters found notable armed clashes in March and August, plus broader regional security pressure, but repeated searches did not surface a late-October/early-November battle surge. September reporting emphasized drone/balloon smuggling interdictions and arrests, which are less likely to become numerous ACLED 'Battles.' So a YES outcome would likely require a genuine regime shift: repeated armed border engagements or major spillover from Syria/Iran-linked actors onto Jordanian territory. That looks very unlikely for Nov 16, still unlikely for Dec 9, and only modestly more plausible by Feb 7 because of longer horizon, winter border-clash risk, and general regional escalation uncertainty.

The current known 360-day baseline is 0.000, so this is effectively a question about whether ACLED will record at least one Strategic development in Hungary in each target 30-day window. That zero baseline is strong evidence that ACLED uses this category very sparingly in Hungary. There are, however, some late-2025 factors that keep the probability from being extremely low: an unusually competitive pre-election environment, major rival rallies, an alleged deepfake incident, media-consolidation moves ahead of the 2026 election, and ongoing Hungary-Ukraine/EU tensions. Even so, comparable mid-2025 sovereignty/rule-of-law developments apparently did not produce any recorded strategic developments in the baseline period, which suggests ACLED often does not code these Hungarian political developments into this category. So I keep all three probabilities below one-third, with later dates moved modestly toward 0.5 because of greater uncertainty and the approaching election season.

But the crucial signal is ACLED-specific: the question notes the most recent known baseline value on 2025-10-30 was still 1.000, which implies zero ACLED fatalities in the preceding 360 days as then published (search_4_summary). That strongly suggests most ordinary homicides are not entering ACLED for Martinique, so a YES likely requires a sudden, clearly ACLED-coded political-violence or organized-armed-violence outbreak producing 11+ fatalities in a single 30-day window. I found no evidence of such a late-2025 outbreak before the cutoff. Near-term is especially low because much of the 11/16 lookback window had already effectively elapsed by the forecast date without signs of mass-fatality unrest. I raise the probability slightly with horizon because there is more time for a new riot/crackdown or organized-violence shock to emerge by February.

I treat this mostly as a question of whether ACLED will record at least one Indonesian fatality in each 30-day window, because the benchmark was reported as 0.000 as of 2025-10-30 and likely remains near zero or at least low enough that a single fatality could be sufficient. The main upside is clear: Reuters, Nikkei, HRW, and ANTARA all describe an active and lethal Papuan insurgency in 2025, with repeated bursts of deaths in April, May, and October. The main downside is mapping those press reports into the resolving ACLED series: despite those reports, the benchmark still appeared to be zero, which suggests either sparse coding/backfill or that many media-reported deaths do not enter this series promptly. For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant window was already partially observed by the forecast date, and I found no clear fatal report after Oct. 17; the best-documented Oct. 16 violence is probably just outside that window, so this date is the lowest. For 2025-12-09 and 2026-02-07, the windows are more like random future months in an active but sporadic conflict environment, so I move somewhat upward but keep all probabilities below 50% because of the ACLED-coding uncertainty. I also keep the furthest date closest to 0.5, per higher horizon uncertainty.

Suriname looks like a very low-base-rate ACLED fatality setting. The strongest evidence is that the known reference average as of late October 2025 was 0.000, so the practical resolution threshold is almost certainly just whether ACLED records any fatality in the target 30-day window. For that threshold to rise above 1 fatality would require very large backfill to the prior 360 days, which seems unlikely. Searches found real but mostly older evidence of lethal unrest in 2023, plus persistent background risks from austerity, illegal mining, corruption, and organized crime. But I did not find clear late-2025 reporting of fresh fatal political violence, and the 2025 election transition appears to have been resolved institutionally via coalition bargaining and parliament electing Jennifer Simons in July, rather than through continuing street unrest. So I put the near-term window at the floor-ish end, with modestly higher probabilities further out because more can change by Dec/Feb and uncertainty should rise with horizon.

Baseline conditions point strongly toward NO. The reference value was 1.000 as of 2025-10-30, so the hurdle is likely about 11 riot events in a 30-day window, perhaps 11-12 if ACLED later revises the baseline slightly. Nicaragua in 2025 appears to be a highly repressive police-state environment with very little visible in-country street mobilization, and Reuters/UN reporting emphasizes consolidation of regime control rather than a growing protest wave. Reuters also reported the macroeconomy was still growing around 4% in early 2025, so there is no clear acute economic-collapse trigger. For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, and there were no signs of an ongoing riot surge, making a crossing especially unlikely. I raise the probability modestly for later dates because there is more time for an exogenous political or economic shock, and Nicaragua has shown in 2018 that unrest can escalate quickly when triggered, but absent evidence of current mobilization the odds remain low.

The reference value was most recently 1.000, so the effective threshold is likely about 11 or more ACLED-coded 'Explosions/Remote violence' events in a 30-day window, possibly a bit higher if backfilled. For North Macedonia, that would require a sharp, unusual militant/terrorist campaign rather than normal background conditions. The strongest evidence points to a very low realized attack rate: US State Department reporting says there were no terrorist attacks in multiple recent years, including 2022, and 2023 reporting focused on preparedness rather than recent violence. AP reporting on the 2022-23 bomb-threat wave describes hoaxes, not actual explosions. I found some evidence of latent risk — notably a December 2024 anti-terror operation against suspects allegedly planning attacks — but not evidence of an ongoing campaign capable of producing 11+ remote-violence events in a month. Searches focused on late 2025 mainly surfaced the accidental nightclub fire and older cases, not a current cluster of explosions. So I put the near-term probability at the floor, with only modest increases for the later dates to reflect greater uncertainty and more room for an unforeseen security shock.

A YES likely requires roughly 11+ ACLED-coded battle events in a 30-day window, because the comparator on 2025-11-09 appears to be about 1.0 and may even be revised upward, which would only make the hurdle harder. Pre-cutoff evidence points strongly to a dormant sovereignty dispute rather than an active conflict: UN and media reporting emphasize peaceful negotiations, Milei explicitly says Argentina does not seek conflict, and 2025 reporting describes UK-Argentina military dialogue rather than force. The only late-2025 signal I found was a UK military exercise in late October, but it appears to have been routine training/protest diplomacy, not kinetic clashes. For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the cutoff with no evidence of battles, so I stay at the floor. I raise the probability modestly for December and February only because longer horizons leave more room for a low-probability interstate incident or miscalculation.

This is essentially a forecast about whether Canada sees at least one ACLED-coded battle in each target 30-day window, because the latest known trailing 360-day 30-day average was 0.000. The base rate is very low: Canada appears to have had no recorded battles in roughly the prior year, and ACLED’s battle definition is narrow, requiring a violent interaction between two organized armed groups. My searches found one plausible pathway — a Quebec anti-government militia plot in July 2025 — but that appears to have been disrupted by arrests months before the forecast date. I found no strong evidence of an active late-2025 Canadian armed-group standoff likely to generate a battle. So I put the near-term probability very low, with modestly higher probabilities further out because there is more time for a new flashpoint to emerge.

Malawi appears to face real political and economic stress, but the bar for YES is high: with the reference value near 1.0, the outcome likely needs about 11 or more ACLED-coded riot events in a 30-day window. The evidence up to 2025-11-09 points to isolated political violence, some clashes, and worsening fuel/economic hardship, but also to a comparatively peaceful 2025 election, early concession by Chakwera, and no clear sign of an imminent nationwide riot wave. That makes the nearest window especially unlikely, since most of it is already effectively in view by the forecast date. I raise probabilities modestly for later dates because uncertainty grows with horizon and chronic shortages plus post-election tensions could still generate a broader unrest episode.

Finland’s recent ACLED baseline is effectively zero, so this is close to asking whether at least one ACLED-coded violence-against-civilians event occurs in each 30-day window. Outside view says that is rare in Finland. The main reason not to go lower is that Finnish authorities had an elevated terrorism threat assessment in 2025 and said the most likely attack would target civilians in a public place. But I found no concrete late-Oct/early-Nov 2025 incident or disrupted plot that would raise the short-run risk, and the notable July 2025 Tampere stabbings were reported as non-political/non-racist, which likely means ordinary criminal violence is not what will drive ACLED VAC here. So the nearest window is lowest because most of it was already observed by the cutoff; the later windows are slightly higher mainly because they are less observed and more uncertain.

I anchor on the FRED DTB1YR statistical output, which estimated P(DTB1YR > 3.49 by 2025-11-16) at 0.61 as of the 2025-11-09 cutoff. The raw FRED/statistical prior (0.71 for being above 2.0 by Nov. 16) is not persuasive here because ECB policy rates are set discretely at Governing Council decisions, not by a smooth time-series process; it is being inflated by last year’s much higher level and a flat recent series. The causal/policy evidence available by 2025-11-09 points strongly against hikes. The ECB had cut from 3.25 to 2.00 over the prior year and then held steady. Reuters polling and reporting around the Oct. 30 decision pointed to a prolonged pause, with some discussion of another cut rather than any hike. That makes an increase by Nov. 16 extremely unlikely, and by Dec. 9 still extremely unlikely. By Feb. 7 there is more time and likely additional meetings, so the probability rises somewhat, but a hike would still require a meaningful upside inflation surprise or sharp hawkish turn that was not the consensus as of the cutoff.

EFFR is mostly a policy-controlled series: it usually changes materially when the FOMC changes the target range, not via large day-to-day drift. The recent realized move before the cutoff was a cut on Oct. 29, with EFFR dropping to about 3.87 and then staying flat. Search evidence shows the next scheduled FOMC decision after Nov. 9 was Dec. 10, so there was no scheduled chance for a hike before Nov. 16 or Dec. 9. Reuters coverage also framed December as cut-versus-hold, not hike. I therefore assign only small probabilities for the first two dates, leaving some residual chance for a tiny technical uptick in the daily EFFR print or an extremely unlikely intermeeting move. By Feb. 7 there is time for the Dec. 10 and late-January meetings, so a surprise re-tightening is more plausible than at the earlier dates, but the broader late-October expectation still pointed toward lower rates into 2026, so the probability remains well below 50%.

Primary credit outstanding is a noisy, event-driven series, so I anchor on the FRED tool’s statistical estimate rather than on a simple trend extrapolation. With the latest known value at 7,138 on 2025-11-05, the tool’s combined short-horizon estimate for exceeding that level by 2025-11-16 is 0.45. I shade slightly lower because the latest print fell from 7,894 to 7,138 and recent momentum is described as flat, which is more consistent with stabilization/mean reversion than a continuing surge. Qualitatively, an Oct. 2 cut in the primary credit rate could support some borrowing persistence, but discount-window usage is still stigmatized and historically large spikes tend to accompany acute stress episodes. Since I do not see strong evidence of a clearly escalating banking-stress event as of the cutoff, I keep the near-term probability a bit below 50%. For the farther dates, uncertainty dominates and the forecast moves closer to 0.5, per the instruction for longer horizons.
