With the latest known value at 7,138 on 2025-11-05, the tool’s combined short-horizon estimate for exceeding that level by 2025-11-16 is 0.45. I shade slightly lower because the latest print fell from 7,894 to 7,138 and recent momentum is described as flat, which is more consistent with stabilization/mean reversion than a continuing surge. Qualitatively, an Oct. 2 cut in the primary credit rate could support some borrowing persistence, but discount-window usage is still stigmatized and historically large spikes tend to accompany acute stress episodes. Since I do not see strong evidence of a clearly escalating banking-stress event as of the cutoff, I keep the near-term probability a bit below 50%. For the farther dates, uncertainty dominates and the forecast moves closer to 0.5, per the instruction for longer horizons.

As of 2025-11-09, I found no evidence that a qualifying broad/global BIS rule had already been implemented between 1 May and 9 Nov 2025. The clearest in-window official action was actually the May 13 move to rescind and not enforce the January AI Diffusion Rule, which cuts against a straightforward YES path. There is still meaningful chance of a late-2025 interim final rule: Commerce simultaneously said it would strengthen semiconductor controls worldwide, and reporting/legal analyses indicated replacement rules were under development, possibly including a global licensing regime and tighter chip thresholds. But with only about seven weeks left in the year and no implemented replacement yet, I judge a late-2025 qualifying rule as possible but less likely than slippage into 2026.

As of 2025-11-09, the strongest evidence points to a NO. Congress had passed TAKE IT DOWN, showing some appetite for narrow AI-harm legislation, but that law likely does not satisfy this market’s criteria because it targets nonconsensual imagery and platform takedowns rather than regulating AI systems or model development. The likeliest remaining qualifying paths appear to be (1) a year-end rider in NDAA/omnibus legislation or (2) a narrow deepfake/digital-replica bill such as NO FAKES. But by fall 2025, NO FAKES still appeared stuck at committee/referral stage, and broader federal AI provisions had been stripped from major vehicles rather than gaining momentum. NDAA contains AI-related provisions, yet many seem focused on DoD internal governance, procurement, export controls, or reporting, which may not clearly satisfy the resolution criteria. With less than two months remaining, including year-end legislative congestion, the chance of a qualifying enactment exists but is small.

As of 2025-11-09, the coalition has substantial planning and many stated contributors, but every authoritative description I found still treats deployment as contingent on hostilities ceasing. Through late October, Reuters and the coalition’s own statement continued to frame the mission as post-ceasefire/postwar, while Russia had rejected an immediate ceasefire and Europe was still working on proposals rather than activating forces. With under two months left in 2025, no evidence of actual deployment orders or advance elements inside Ukraine, and only a narrow fallback path via air/naval components, I put the chance of a qualifying coalition deployment by Dec. 31 at 6%.

As of 2025-11-09, I think NO is more likely. Europe has clear precedent for donating HIMARS-compatible launchers to Ukraine, and some countries are recapitalizing with PULS or other systems after earlier donations. But after several targeted searches, I found no clear 2025 Reuters/AP or official-source report of a new qualifying European commitment of launchers or substitute systems. The visible 2025 focus appears to be on missiles/ammunition, shells, and broader funding rather than new launcher transfers. With only about seven weeks left in the year, and with evidence that European states are replenishing depleted rocket-artillery inventories, the remaining path to YES is mainly a surprise year-end package or an EU procurement decision specifically naming launchers. That is plausible, but not the most likely outcome.

Most of the mass is now on NO. The strongest evidence is that after Ukraine transit ended, Russia’s only meaningful routes into the EU were TurkStream pipeline gas plus LNG, and Bruegel-based reporting put total EU imports from Russia at only about 27.6 bcm for the first nine months of 2025. That makes it very unlikely any of Q1-Q3 reached 15 bcm, so YES would require a large Q4 spike to at least 15 bcm. Given the lower 2025 supply base, limited pipeline route capacity, and only modest evidence of an autumn surge, I think such a spike is possible but clearly unlikely.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the strongest plausible path to a YES looked like a renewed Iran-Israel/U.S. military escalation leading Iran to selectively interdict Western shipping rather than a literal total blockade. But the key evidence points the other way: even during the intense June 2025 war scare, official maritime reporting said traffic was still flowing, and post-ceasefire shipping traffic/rates partially normalized rather than deteriorating toward closure. Iran clearly retained capability and some officials kept threatening restrictions, so the risk was not negligible, but after the main mid-year danger spike passed without actual closure, the remaining seven-week probability looked closer to the mid-teens than the 20%+ range.

By Nov. 9, 2025, the clearest identified path to Musk leaving Tesla in 2025 had largely closed: shareholders approved his compensation package with over 75% support, despite prior board warnings that rejection could cause him to walk away. Tesla's chair also publicly denied that the board was actively seeking a replacement, and Musk had said earlier in 2025 that he intended to remain CEO for at least five more years. The remaining ways this resolves YES are mostly unforeseen shocks—legal, health, financing, or an abrupt reversal—which are real but relatively unlikely over the short time remaining in the year.

As of 2025-11-09, Hamilton appears to trail Leclerc by a large margin with only three races and one sprint left. Leclerc was already 46 points ahead after Singapore, outscored Hamilton again in Mexico, and remained ahead after São Paulo; combined with Leclerc’s much stronger 2025 form (seven podiums to Hamilton’s none), Hamilton likely needs an unusually large late swing plus multiple poor Leclerc results to finish ahead.

I estimate a 10% chance of a YES resolution. The strongest update is that even after the major June 2025 US strikes on Iranian nuclear sites, the situation de-escalated into a ceasefire and resumed bargaining rather than an openly sustained US-Iran war. By late June Reuters was describing a fragile ceasefire, direct US-Iran contacts, and Trump explicitly rejecting regime change; by November 7 Reuters was still reporting sanction-lifting discussions rather than active warfare. Late-September snapback/IAEA tensions were real but were being handled through sanctions and diplomatic confrontation, not a US military campaign. With less than two months remaining as of the cutoff, the base rate for crossing from hostility into a publicly acknowledged, sustained war meeting this market’s criteria is low, though not negligible because proxy attacks or a sudden nuclear crisis could still trigger escalation.

I’m at the minimum floor. By the question’s own criteria, a YES requires two consecutive negative GDP quarters both in 2025. BEA’s Q2 2025 advance release shows real GDP increased 3.0%, so Q1-Q2 cannot qualify (search_1_result_2). A BEA result for Q3 2025 indicates real GDP increased 4.1% (search_1_result_4). If Q3 was positive, then neither Q2-Q3 nor Q3-Q4 can be two consecutive negative quarters. That makes YES effectively impossible based on information available by 2025-11-09, aside from tiny residual risk from source/criteria ambiguity.

I put this at 9%. By 2025-11-09, the strongest evidence is that NBER still had not dated any post-2020 peak, and its official business-cycle chronology still ended with the Feb 2020 peak / Apr 2020 trough. Meanwhile, most of the indicators NBER emphasizes most heavily—especially payroll employment and real personal income less transfers—were still rising through much of 2024, even as the Sahm Rule triggered and industrial production weakened. That leaves a nonzero chance that late revisions or a borderline late-2024 turning point could prompt a last-minute retrospective call before end-2025, but it looks unlikely.

I estimate a 10% chance. The case for YES is that the official WR is already very close to the barrier at 2:00:35, Valencia is a fast legal course set up for aggressive pacing, and both Jacob Kiplimo and Sabastian Sawe showed in 2025 that elite runners can hold near-record pace deep into races. But the strongest contrary evidence is that no legal marathon in 2025 has even broken 2:02, recent record-chasing efforts in Berlin and Chicago still ended around 2:02 despite ideal intent, and with the calendar nearly over the path to YES likely runs through essentially one remaining race. That makes a legal sub-2 by Dec. 31, 2025 a real but still low-probability tail event.

By the Nov. 9 cutoff, the event had almost certainly not already happened again: Reuters-based evidence shows April 10 was only about -3.46%, October 10 was -2.71%, and November 4 was -1.17%, with October described as the biggest one-day drop since April 10. So the forecast is really about the final ~7 weeks of 2025. In that remaining window, a >5% close-to-close S&P 500 drop is a genuine tail event. The strongest counterweight is that 2025 had already seen one such crash day on April 4 and still carried policy/macro shock risk. But by early November the market had recovered sharply, was up strongly from the April low and up year-to-date, and Reuters described only episodic turbulence rather than an ongoing panic regime. That combination points to a low-but-not-tiny chance of a fresh extreme selloff before year-end.

As of the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the strongest public FrontierMath results are still far below 80%: Epoch reported Gemini 2.5 Deep Think at 29% on Tiers 1–3 and 10% on Tier 4, while GPT-5 Pro reached 13% on Tier 4. Earlier independent Epoch results had top systems only in the mid-to-high teens overall/substantially below 25%. Given that FrontierMath is private, expert-created, and periodically refreshed to resist contamination and saturation, jumping to >80% before Dec. 31, 2025 would require a dramatic discontinuity in capability or a major change in benchmark definition. That seems possible but very unlikely.

I estimate a 3.5% chance the 49ers win Super Bowl LX as of 2025-11-09. The key outside-view anchor is about 3.1% for any NFL team, and current evidence leaves San Francisco only slightly above that. On the positive side, they were still very much alive around the cutoff, reaching 6-3 before the Nov. 9 Rams game, with Christian McCaffrey playing at an elite level and Mac Jones keeping them afloat while Brock Purdy was out (search_3_result_5, search_3_result_4, search_3_result_7). On the negative side, the market had already downgraded them to roughly 13th overall/7th in the NFC in Super Bowl odds by early November (search_0_summary), and the injury load was severe: Purdy had missed extensive time, several pass-catchers were banged up, and reports indicated Nick Bosa and Fred Warner were done for the season (search_1_summary, search_3_summary). The Nov. 9 loss to the Rams likely dropped them to 6-4 and further hurt their division/seeding path (search_3_result_1). Taken together, this looks like a fringe-to-mid playoff contender with enough talent to matter, but not a top-tier title favorite.

By the cutoff, BTC had clearly been in a powerful 2025 bull market, making repeated all-time highs and reaching above $125k in early October, with strong ETF inflows supporting the move. That keeps a $150k touch alive. But the more recent evidence points to cooled momentum: Reuters reported a drop to about $104.8k in mid-October amid trade-war stress, another Reuters piece described a historic 24-hour crypto wipeout and surge in hedging, and early-November snippets place BTC around/below $110k with ETF inflows only just turning positive again after six days of outflows. From roughly $110k, BTC would need about a 35-36% rally in ~7 weeks to touch $150k. BTC is volatile enough that this is far from impossible, especially since November is historically strong, but options commentary showing activity centered near $110k and active downside hedging suggests the market was not heavily pricing a late-2025 run to $150k. Net: plausible, but still more likely No than Yes.

By Nov. 9, 2025, the evidence pointed to a real ESPN/NFL transaction, but not one likely to put the NFL Media unit under Disney control by Dec. 31, 2025. The positive case is strong on intent: Disney/ESPN and the NFL publicly announced a deal for ESPN to acquire NFL Network and other media assets, and NFL owners approved it in October. But the negative case is stronger on timing and scope: the agreement was described as non-binding and still subject to definitive agreements and regulatory approvals; major reporting said review could take 9-12 months or even until late 2026; and the NFL was retaining important media businesses like NFL Films, NFL+, NFL.com, team sites, and parts of RedZone control. So, as of the forecast date, YES required either an unusually fast close or a loose interpretation of 'under Disney’s control.' Both seemed possible but clearly less likely than NO.

Waymo had become visible enough in 2025 to be sketch material: it was scaling rapidly, testing in New York, and had a few inherently comic viral incidents. But the stronger evidence is on the NO side: by the Nov. 9 cutoff there is no obvious sign that SNL had already done a Waymo sketch, the confirmed late-2025 schedule only clearly showed one remaining November episode beyond the cutoff, and SNL would often be more likely to parody generic robotaxis or Tesla than the specific brand name Waymo. With only a small number of episodes left in 2025, I estimate about a 12% chance of a Waymo-featured skit before year-end.

Chrome appears overwhelmingly likely to remain the most popular browser at end-2025. Multiple pre-cutoff 2025 sources summarizing StatCounter/Wikipedia-style global browser data put Chrome around 65-67% worldwide, with Safari roughly 14-19% and Edge around 5%, and they describe the ranking as stable and even strengthening in late 2025. With less than two months from the Nov. 9 cutoff to year-end, overturning a lead of ~45+ percentage points would require either an unprecedented collapse or a major methodology/source change. I leave a small residual chance for resolution-source quirks or a sudden late-year shock, but YES is by far the most likely outcome.

As of 2025-11-09, the strongest evidence is that Musk loudly announced the 'America Party' in July, but did not back it with verified infrastructure. AP said it was unclear he had actually formed anything, fake FEC filings were disavowed, Axios said the effort was 'failing to launch,' and Reuters later reported he was putting the brakes on it to focus on his companies. With only ~7 weeks left, YES is still possible because the bar is subjective and Musk could create a minimal website/account quickly, but NO is more likely.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the strongest recent evidence points to a real thaw after a very deep summer rupture. The downside case is clear: in June Trump and Musk had a spectacular public breakup, with Trump saying the relationship was over and warning of consequences, and the feud resurfaced in July around Musk’s political moves. But the more recent evidence matters more for an end-of-year state question: Musk publicly regretted some posts, the two were seen shaking hands and chatting at Charlie Kirk’s memorial service in September, and Trump then confirmed in late October that they had been 'chatting again' since September, with reporting describing Musk as back in Trump’s good graces. Since the resolution is subjective and asks only about year-end status, that late-October rapprochement pushes the forecast slightly above even odds. I keep it well below certainty because both men are unusually volatile and there were still unresolved policy/political tensions that could trigger another falling-out before Dec. 31.

I estimate a 6% chance of YES. Trump had both motive and demonstrated willingness to pressure Paramount/CBS in 2025, which keeps the risk above zero. But the specific South Park controversy appears to have produced only a White House denunciation, while legal experts said a direct claim over this parody would be implausible. Most importantly, by late September and mid-October mainstream coverage was still discussing the satire and fallout without reporting any South Park-specific lawsuit, cease-and-desist, or policy retaliation. Given the long period of inaction after a very public provocation, the remaining hazard by Dec. 31 looks low, though not negligible because Trump could still attempt a low-merit, headline-grabbing move.

Iran faces real stress—economic contraction, renewed sanctions, aftereffects of the June war with Israel, and the possibility of renewed protests. But as of 2025-11-09, the evidence points much more toward regime resilience than imminent collapse. Reuters reported no significant anti-regime protests after the June war and described a rapid, effective internal crackdown with arrests, checkpoints, and deployments; later reporting showed officials worried about unrest but still in control of central institutions. Succession planning around Khamenei appears designed to preserve continuity, not signal disintegration, and analysts saw no imminent coup. The opposition remains fragmented and activists were wary of mobilizing, while experts expected the IRGC to consolidate rather than defect in a crisis. Given the very short remaining horizon to year-end and the lack of visible loss of control in Tehran, regime change by 2025 looks possible but distinctly unlikely.

By 2025-11-09, the path to YES looked narrow. Q1 2025 appears to have been negative, but Q2 2025 rebounded strongly and Q4 2024 was positive, knocking out the obvious earlier two-quarter sequences. The remaining realistic route was a Q3-Q4 2025 double-negative, yet late-2025 nowcasts/forecasts pointed to positive Q3 growth. I leave some residual probability for an unexpectedly negative/revised Q3 plus a weak Q4, and for small ambiguity about whether a Q4 2025 start could matter operationally, but overall NO was much more likely.

I estimate a 9% chance. By Nov. 9, 2025, the strongest obvious revival path had largely closed: Nate Silver said he does not plan to reacquire the FiveThirtyEight name and that Disney would not sell it to him specifically. Meanwhile, key 538 forecasting IP appears to have left with Silver, leaving Disney with a weaker asset centered more on the name/archive than the signature models. March 2025 reporting described a full shutdown: the entire staff was laid off, publishing stopped, and ABC planned to continue polling/data analysis elsewhere rather than under the 538 label. With less than two months left in the year and no surfaced reporting of buyers, transfers, or revival plans, YES now seems a low-probability tail outcome. The main remaining paths are a quiet sale/licensing deal or a small symbolic Disney revival attempt before year-end.

I estimate a 14% chance. The main negative is that, by Nov. 9, 2025, independent sources still described Manifold as a play-money platform, and a Feb. 2025 industry piece quoted Stephen Grugett saying the free-to-play model helps Manifold avoid real-money regulation and immature crypto infrastructure. I also could not find any concrete public evidence of a 2025 affiliate launch, licensing progress, or announced real-money/crypto rollout. The main positive is the question background that Stevo had mentioned exploring crypto again for a separate set of markets, plus a vague X bio hinting at a ‘next chapter.’ Because the resolution only requires one jurisdiction or an affiliated platform, a surprise late-year crypto launch is not impossible, but with only ~7 weeks left and no clear public trail, it looks unlikely.

I estimate a 17% chance of YES. The key official evidence is the BLS/FRED federal payroll series trajectory: federal government employment was reported down 69,000 from the January 2025 peak by June, 84,000 by July, and 97,000 by August. That leaves a required further drop of more than 153,000 in the remaining months to exceed a 250,000 decline by before January 2026—an acceleration far beyond the pace seen so far. This is made harder because the resolution uses the broad FEDGOV payroll series including USPS, and because BLS payroll counts can still include workers on paid leave. News by Oct. 2025 also pointed to rehiring/reinstatements and only about 4,200 workers affected by formal RIF notices so far, which cuts against a massive late-2025 collapse. Main residual uncertainty is that I do not have the latest official Sept/Oct series value as of Nov. 9, and there could still be delayed buyout/RIF effects in Nov-Dec.

My estimate is 14%. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, Musk had already made the public-announcement half easy to satisfy, but the decisive issue is whether he would complete authentic FEC paperwork by Dec. 31. The evidence up to the cutoff points strongly to delay or de-prioritization rather than imminent legal formation: early purported filings were fake, major outlets still reported no valid filing, CNN said there were few concrete steps even by August, and Reuters reported Musk was quietly putting the brakes on the effort to focus on his companies. With less than two months remaining at the forecast date, a YES would require a late reversal and quick filing. Musk's wealth and impulsiveness keep that possibility alive, but it looks clearly less likely than not.

By the Nov. 9 cutoff, the strongest evidence suggests the market’s key condition had already been met: multiple major outlets reported that suspects were physically apprehended on Oct. 25-26, and later reporting consistently specified that at least some of those in custody were believed to be direct participants in the robbery itself. Most importantly, the New York Times reported that three of the four people in custody were suspected of having directly robbed the museum and were charged accordingly (search_2_result_0), while Reuters reported the first two detainees partially admitted involvement (search_0_result_8). The Guardian and NYT also said three of the four robbers were believed arrested/in custody (search_0_result_1, search_0_result_6). There is some residual risk because some later-arrested suspects denied involvement and some peripheral detainees were released (search_1_result_2, search_1_result_8), and the market creator allows time for reporting to disconfirm an arrest. But the core prosecutor-backed narrative before the cutoff strongly points to at least one actual physical robber already being in custody. So YES is very likely, though not certain.

I put this at 6%. The outside view is very unfavorable: through 2024 there were effectively no $100M+ US theatrical releases publicly acknowledged as written by or substantially script-assisted by AI. By Nov. 9, 2025, trade coverage shows AI is entering Hollywood workflows, including some script-shaping and blockbuster-franchise development discussion, but the strongest blockbuster-specific public use cases are still VFX/cost-cutting rather than screenplay authorship. The 2023 WGA deal also raises legal/reputational friction around AI-generated writing and public disclosure. Most importantly, despite targeted searches in major trade outlets, I found no specific qualifying $100M+ film already released or scheduled for late 2025 with a concrete public acknowledgment of significant AI script assistance. With only a short window left in 2025, resolution would likely require an unexpected late disclosure by one of a handful of conservative studio tentpoles, which seems possible but unlikely.

I put this at 30%. By 2025-11-09, frontier models looked plausibly above a generally educated human on many benchmarked cognitive tasks, with very strong scores on hard reasoning tests and rapidly improving reliability. But the key resolution is a poll about whether AGI 'currently exists,' so social consensus matters more than raw capability. The strongest late-2025 evidence still suggested important brittleness/hallucination problems and, crucially, even many bullish insiders were placing true AGI beyond end-2025 rather than saying it had already arrived. With only ~7 weeks left from the forecast date, I see a meaningful but minority chance that December releases or shifting sentiment push the poll to YES.

Virgin Galactic looks financially weak but not on the verge of a bankruptcy filing before 2026. The strongest evidence against an imminent filing is liquidity: it had over $500M in cash/cash equivalents/marketable securities as of June 30, 2025, with guided Q3 free-cash-flow burn of roughly $100–110M, which still implies substantial remaining cash into late 2025 (search_1_summary). Management also described the balance sheet as strong in August 2025 (search_1_summary). On the other hand, the company clearly faced real financing stress in mid-2025, including covenant waivers and a short bridge-loan extension with Madryn, while operations remained paused and meaningful revenue was not expected until 2026 (search_0_summary). That combination makes bankruptcy a live medium-term risk, but over the very short remaining horizon to Jan. 1, 2026, absent evidence of a fresh default, failed financing, or a going-concern alarm, filing probability seems modest rather than high.

China clearly wants additional overseas logistics/basing access, and several BRICS members overlap with the US list of candidate hosts. But the decisive evidence is negative: as of late 2024 DoD still identified only prospective sites in BRICS countries, not an operational base; the strongest BRICS case, the UAE/Khalifa Port project, appears to have been halted under US pressure; and the March 2025 ODNI threat assessment still described BRICS-relevant locations as under consideration rather than operating. With less than two months left until Jan. 1, 2026 and resolution requiring a public announcement by China or the US intelligence community, a YES remains possible but unlikely.

I put this at 6%. The outside view is harsh: successful Swiss Federal Tribunal challenges to CAS awards are rare, and the Swiss court reviews only narrow procedural defects rather than the sporting merits. This case does have some real pro-Chiles features — a live appeal, alleged on-time video evidence, and colorable ‘right to be heard’ / conflict-of-interest claims — so it is not near zero. But as of the latest pre-cutoff reporting the appeal was still pending with no clear timetable, leaving only a short window before 2026 for both a ruling and an overturn. Overall, the combination of low historical success rates and late-stage timing risk dominates.

I estimate a 7% chance. The main driver is that every identifiable CDC/H5 IRAT update through mid-2025 still left H5 viruses in the moderate emergence-risk range, not close enough in public reporting to suggest an imminent jump to the >=8.0 'high' threshold. The May 2025 CDC assessments added two more U.S. H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b viruses and reporting says they ranked slightly below the earlier Texas strain, while all five assessed H5 viruses remained moderate. Combined with continued reporting of no sustained human-to-human transmission and unchanged low public risk, the path to a high emergence score before Jan. 1, 2026 appears narrow. The residual 7% reflects that H5 remains biologically concerning, CDC can update IRAT on short notice, and a late-2025 cluster or new transmission evidence could still trigger a sharp reassessment.

I estimate a 94% chance of YES. The outside-view base rate is already high because yearly worldwide box-office champions are usually franchise films. The inside view makes it stronger: Ne Zha 2 was already reported by multiple pre-cutoff sources as the biggest film of 2025 at roughly $2.1B-$2.2B, and it is clearly a sequel/franchise entry (search_2_summary). The plausible late-year challengers are also franchise films—especially Avatar: Fire and Ash, Zootopia 2, and Wicked: For Good (search_3_summary). That means YES is favored under either of the two plausible readings of the question: if it resolves on the late-November year-to-date leader, Ne Zha 2 is overwhelmingly likely to qualify; if it somehow uses full-year 2025 final grosses, the most likely films to overtake it are still franchise movies. The remaining 6% reflects residual ambiguity in the question wording/date mismatch and a small chance of an unexpected non-franchise breakout.

Rockstar/Take-Two officially moved GTA VI out of 2025. Major outlets reported on May 2, 2025 that the game was delayed to May 26, 2026, and Rockstar’s own Newswire later listed an even later date of November 19, 2026. Since Europe would normally be included in any standard global console launch, the question effectively reduces to whether the game launches anywhere in 2025. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the strongest available evidence says it does not. Only a very unusual edge case—some authorized European sale inconsistent with Rockstar’s announced launch date—keeps this above zero.

Spain already appears very likely to be ahead on the exact resolution metric. By late October 2025, Reuters reported Spain’s Q3 2025 real GDP was up 2.8% y/y (search_2_result_3). Separate Eurostat-based reporting put euro-area Q3 2025 real GDP growth at 1.3% y/y (search_4_result_5). Combined with Spain’s stronger q/q growth in Q3 2025, 0.6% versus 0.2% for the euro area (search_0_result_1; search_1_summary), the gap is about 1.5 percentage points. Ordinary revisions are very unlikely to erase that, though not impossible, and there is a small residual risk from methodological alignment or later revisions before resolution.

By Nov. 9, 2025, the key enacted vehicle was the July 4, 2025 OBBBA. Some post-enactment analyses used language like 'domestic content parity' and discussed 40/45/50/55% thresholds, which creates real ambiguity. But the stronger overall read is that the enacted law primarily imposed FEOC/material-assistance restrictions and changed phaseouts/deadlines, while domestic-content percentages still operated as bonus-adder rules rather than as a universal prerequisite for any 45Y credit. That interpretation is reinforced by commentary explicitly describing the higher percentages as needed to obtain the 10% domestic-content bonus adder. Since a dramatic shift from optional bonus to mandatory base-credit condition would likely have been highlighted much more clearly across summaries, I think YES is possible but unlikely.

I estimate a 29% chance the Court grants cert before Jan. 15, 2026. The strongest positive is the June 30, 2025 CVSG: the Court does not invite the Solicitor General in routine low-interest petitions, and this case presents a genuine, high-stakes federal-preemption split with major nationwide consequences. The split appears materially stronger than in Monsanto’s prior failed cert effort because the Third Circuit’s 2024 Schaffner decision now conflicts with the Ninth and Eleventh Circuits (search_1_summary). That said, several factors keep me below one-third. First, the Court previously denied review in similar Roundup litigation, and in the earlier Hardeman episode the Solicitor General advised denial (search_1_summary), which suggests at least some institutional resistance to this issue. Second, Justice Alito’s recusal slightly raises the practical hurdle by leaving only eight participating justices (search_2_result_5). Third, and most important for this market, timing is tight: as of the Nov. 9, 2025 forecast date I could verify the CVSG but not a filed SG merits recommendation or conference setting, so even a grant-worthy petition may not be acted on before mid-January. Net: clearly stronger than an ordinary cert petition, but still more likely than not to miss this specific deadline or be denied.

Russia had not conducted a nuclear explosive test by the Nov. 9 cutoff, leaving only about seven weeks to Jan. 1. The risk is clearly above a normal baseline because Putin on Nov. 5 ordered proposals for resuming testing, Russian officials say Novaya Zemlya is ready on short notice, and experts think a simple political demonstration shot could be faster than a full scientific test. But the strongest evidence still points to signaling and contingency planning rather than an already-made decision: recent activity at Novaya Zemlya was a Burevestnik missile launch, not a nuclear detonation; Kremlin statements emphasized that neither Russia nor China had resumed testing; and Russia has often framed any move as reciprocal to U.S. testing. That reciprocal path looks weak in the forecast window because Reuters reported the U.S. was not planning nuclear explosions at this time and experts said resuming contained underground U.S. tests would take far longer than the remaining weeks. Net: a real but still low chance of a Russian test before Jan. 1, 2026.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, BTC had clearly been in a strong bull market and reached a fresh ATH above $125k in early October, so $170k was not absurdly far away. But the more recent evidence points to a sharp October washout to about $104.8k, the first October monthly loss since 2018, and options/consensus focus nearer $140k-$150k than $170k. With roughly seven weeks left, a Binance intraminute wick to $170k was still plausible, but it looked more like a meaningful tail outcome than the central expectation.

I estimate a 5% chance. The market needs a formal public Hamas commitment to disarm by Dec. 31, and the best late evidence before the Nov. 9 cutoff points the other way: in a Reuters interview on Oct. 19, senior Hamas official Mohammed Nazzal said Hamas couldn't commit to disarm and wanted to retain a security role in Gaza; AFP-reported comments on Oct. 11 called disarmament 'out of the question'; and Reuters on Nov. 3-5 still described disarmament as a major unresolved obstacle and said Hamas had not stated it would agree to disarm. There is still a small residual chance because Hamas had entered a first-phase framework whose end-state included demilitarization, and some expert reporting suggested private openness to partial or staged decommissioning. But with less than two months left, and with the issue apparently deferred and publicly resisted, a qualifying official announcement looks very unlikely.

By Nov. 9, 2025, the arithmetic is overwhelmingly against YES. The year started with the upper bound at 4.50% after the Dec. 2024 cut (search_2_result_5). By the Oct. 29, 2025 meeting, the range was 3.75%-4.00% after a second consecutive 25 bp cut, meaning only 50 bp of 2025 easing had occurred so far (search_0_result_4). October was the seventh of eight meetings, so only December remained (search_0_result_4). Therefore YES would require roughly a 150 bp cut at the final scheduled meeting. But late-October reporting said a December cut was 'in doubt,' with futures pricing centered on a standard 25 bp move (67.9% chance) and even the dovish dissenter calling for only 50 bp, not 150 bp (search_2_result_1; search_2_result_6). Absent a sudden crisis, an outcome this large is near the floor.

A 50+ bp cut at the scheduled January 2026 FOMC meeting looks like a tail outcome as of 2025-11-09. The strongest evidence points to gradual 25 bp easing or even a pause: Reuters' late-October economist poll expected 25 bp cuts at the next two meetings, not 50s, and only 2 of 117 expected a 50 bp December move (search_0_result_2). Powell said in September there was not widespread support for a 50 bp cut (search_0_result_7). After the October 29 meeting, Powell said a December cut was 'not assured,' inflation was still above target, and Reuters reported analysts debating whether December might even be the last cut of the cycle (search_2_result_0, search_2_result_2, search_2_result_6). Early-November private forecasts also pointed to smaller, later cuts—Goldman expected 25 bp in December and then March/June 2026, while RBC saw no further 2025 cuts and only two 25 bp cuts in 2026 (search_1_result_7, search_1_result_4). There is some upside risk because the labor market had softened enough to trigger September/October cuts and 2026 policy was described as very unclear (search_0_result_0, search_0_result_2), but absent a recession scare or financial stress, a jumbo scheduled January cut is unlikely. I therefore put this at about 9%.

Bournazel is a real candidate, not a fringe one: by late October 2025 he had the backing of Renaissance plus Horizons, which gives him organization and legitimacy. But the much more important evidence is electoral position. Late-2025 polling puts him only around 14-15%, well behind Rachida Dati and also behind the main left candidate, and one reported three-way second-round scenario has the unified left winning with Bournazel in the race. Recent reporting also suggests the Renaissance choice did not unify the center/right but instead formalized a split with Dati. With the 2025 reform making the Paris contest more directly citywide, weak topline polling is harder to dismiss as an arrondissement artifact. His plausible winning path seems to require a major late realignment—especially Dati fading or withdrawing in his favor—which is possible but not likely. So I put his chance at about 6% as of 2025-11-09.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, there was no sign Trump had already named Powell’s successor, but the most recent and most authoritative reporting pointed toward a late-2025 choice rather than deferral into 2026. Reuters reported an increasingly advanced process over the summer and fall: Trump had narrowed the field to a few names, Bessent said finalists would be presented for December interviews, and on Oct. 27 Trump said a decision might be made by year-end. Bloomberg/Axios also reported Bessent expected a selection before year-end. The main counterweight is that a 2025 announcement was optional, not necessary: the administration could wait for an early-2026 Board vacancy or until Powell’s May 2026 chair-term expiration. So 'no announcement by Dec. 31' remains quite plausible, but on balance less likely than an announcement.

As of 2025-11-09, this looks like a real contender but still an underdog. The album has already done the hard first step—debuting at No. 1 with a historic opening and extending that to a five-week streak—and its weekly declines appear to be moderating rather than collapsing. But the bar is not merely a long run; it is 18 uninterrupted weeks, which is an exceptionally rare Billboard 200 feat. The best recent Taylor comp in the evidence is The Tortured Poets Department, which managed 12 straight weeks, and even in the 21st century only a small number of albums reach double-digit weeks at No. 1 at all. From week 5, it still needs 13 more consecutive wins through mid-February, meaning a lot of exposure to holiday-chart volatility and possible new-release shocks. Balancing the extraordinary start against the extreme rarity of the target, I put YES at 16%.

As of 2025-11-09, a March 2026 hold looks slightly more likely than not. The key balance is: (1) the Fed was already in an easing cycle after September and October 2025 cuts, which raises the chance of another move at any given meeting; but (2) the September 2025 SEP/dot plot pointed to only one cut in all of 2026, some forecasters explicitly expected a hold until June 2026, and the October meeting appears to have made even a December 2025 cut less certain. That combination suggests a slower, more intermittent easing cadence rather than automatic cuts at every meeting. Markets still priced more 2026 easing than the dots implied, and labor-market weakness could still force a March cut, so 'no change' is only a modest favorite, not a strong one.

By Nov. 9, 2025, no Article 5 invocation had occurred, and the strongest 2025 stress tests of NATO—Poland shooting down Russian drones after repeated airspace breaches, Estonia's Russian jet incursion, and later Lithuanian incidents—were all handled with Article 4 consultations, warnings, protests, and deterrent signaling rather than collective-defense invocation. That strongly suggests NATO's political threshold for Article 5 remained very high. The main remaining path to YES is a new, clearly attributable, mass-casualty or otherwise unmistakable armed attack on a NATO member in the final seven weeks of 2025. That's possible given elevated Russia-related tensions and NATO's explicit statement that some hybrid attacks could qualify, but still unlikely.

I estimate an 11% chance. The bullish case is that Altman’s 2025 rhetoric came unusually close to the line—most notably saying OpenAI is now confident it knows how to build AGI (search_1_result_7). But the stronger, more recent evidence points the other way: late-October reporting had OpenAI emphasizing future scale and infrastructure rather than claiming AGI already exists (search_1_result_3); OpenAI’s revised Microsoft deal now routes AGI determinations through an independent expert panel, raising the bar and reducing the incentive to make a loose public declaration (search_2_result_0; search_2_result_3; search_2_result_4); and OpenAI’s biggest public event of the fall, DevDay 2025, highlighted GPT-5 Pro, agents, and productization with no AGI framing (search_4_result_1; lookup_0). With only ~7 weeks left from the forecast date, an AGI announcement is possible, especially via Altman or a blog post, but it looks unlikely.

WHO pandemic declarations are extremely rare, and by the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff there is no sign WHO had already declared any new pandemic in 2025. The specific February HKU5-CoV-2 story looks much less threatening on closer inspection: it involved lab evidence of cell entry, but reporting said there were no known human infections and experts did not view it as an immediate public-health threat. The other plausible late-2025 routes to a WHO pandemic declaration also look weak: mpox was being monitored as an emergency without sustained global community transmission outside Africa, and H5N1 still lacked reported human-to-human transmission. With less than two months left in the resolution window, the residual chance of a sudden novel outbreak severe enough for WHO to use the exceptional word 'pandemic' is small but not zero.

Best proxy available before the NASA release is Copernicus/ERA5, which reported October 2025 as the third-warmest October on record. That makes a very warm NASA GISTEMP outcome more likely than not. However, October 2025 was not a record, had notable regional cool anomalies, and was only marginally above lower-ranked warm Octobers in Copernicus, so the 1.10°C line still looks fairly close rather than decisively busted. With cross-dataset differences and no direct NASA October 2025 value yet, I put the chance of NASA coming in below 1.10°C at a bit under 40%.

Multiple independent pre-cutoff reports indicate that Larry Ellison overtook Elon Musk on the Bloomberg Billionaires Index on Sept. 10, 2025, during the resolution window. The strongest summarized evidence says Bloomberg had Ellison at $393B vs. Musk at $385B at 10 a.m. ET that day, even though Musk apparently regained the lead by market close. Because the market resolves YES if Musk is anything other than #1 at any point in the period, that reported intraday flip would already satisfy the criterion. Residual uncertainty is only about source/reporting precision, not the underlying direction of evidence.

Moonshot looks like a real contender but an outside shot. The best direct evidence on the resolution source is negative: Kimi-K2-0905 was only 7th in September (search_3_result_1), standard K2 previews were around #11–12 on Nov. 8, and K2 Thinking was not yet listed (search_3_result_0). That matters a lot because the horizon is short and rivals like Google/OpenAI/Anthropic/xAI were already dominating late-2025 discussions and earlier Arena standings (search_2_result_8, search_2_result_2). The bullish case is that Moonshot has shown strong momentum—top open-source Arena placement and strong recent benchmark results for K2 Thinking (search_1_result_3, search_1_result_2)—so a fast jump after listing is plausible. But to resolve YES, Moonshot likely needs a newly listed model to leapfrog several entrenched leaders within ~7 weeks. That seems possible but not likely.

I estimate a low probability because the best pre-release proxy is too warm for this bracket. Copernicus reported October 2025 at 1.55°C above 1850–1900 and as the third-warmest October on record, which likely maps to a NASA GISTEMP anomaly above 1.14°C once translated to NASA’s 1951–1980 baseline. A contemporaneous NASA-citing source said October 2024, the second-hottest October, was 1.32°C above the 1951–1980 average, so October 2025 being third-hottest suggests it is probably somewhat below 1.32°C but still more likely in the low/mid-1.2s than in 1.10–1.14.

Biden looks like a long shot for exactly #2 on Google’s 2025 global Trending People list. The strongest pro-Biden fact is his May 2025 metastatic prostate cancer diagnosis, which clearly drove a major search spike and widespread public curiosity (search_1_result_0, search_1_result_5, search_1_result_9). But Google’s Year in Search is based on biggest year-over-year spikes, not raw fame, and that tends to favor deaths, scandals, and breakout figures (search_0_summary). Historically, Biden was only #5 globally in 2024 even though the U.S. election was the year’s top trending news story (search_0_summary), which is a meaningful warning sign. Meanwhile, several 2025 rivals seem to fit the 'trending spike' template better: Pope Leo XIV and Pope Francis-related events appear to have generated massive global attention, and pre-cutoff commentary also points to Bianca Censori as an unusually strong breakout candidate after a huge one-day search surge around the Grammys (search_3_summary; search_3_result_1, search_3_result_2, search_3_result_6). So Biden has a plausible path to the top 10 and maybe even top 5, but exactly #2 appears unlikely.

I put Crystal Palace at the very bottom of the allowed range. The outside view is extremely harsh: Premier League titles almost always go to a tiny set of elite clubs, and Palace have never won the English top flight. The in-season evidence did show they were better than a typical midtable side — ESPN noted a strong adjusted goal differential and Palace were only a couple of points off the upper chasing pack (search_1_result_9), bookmakers had shortened them to 66/1 by late September after a strong run (search_3_result_0), and Opta gave them a 3.92% title chance after Matchday 6 (search_4_result_3). But the more important late-cutoff evidence points strongly against a real title bid: ESPN on Nov. 4 described Arsenal, Man City, and Liverpool as the clear top three and projected Palace to finish on only 53.8 points, with the 9th-best rest-of-season outlook (search_1_result_9). Opta’s Oct. 29 update had Arsenal, City, and Liverpool combining for 92.2% of title probability, leaving just 7.8% for the entire rest of the league (search_4_result_6). So while Palace were a credible European-place outsider, they were not a credible title favorite by 2025-11-09. My unconstrained estimate would be a bit below 5%, but under the platform rules I submit 5%.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the best available evidence suggests BTC had not come close to $50k during the resolution window: Reuters-covered April weakness put the 2025 low around $74k, autumn 2025 saw fresh all-time highs near $125k-$126k, and near the cutoff BTC was still about $102k after only a temporary break below $100k. From that level, YES requires an additional ~51% drop in under two months. Bitcoin is capable of violent selloffs, but moves of that magnitude over such a short horizon usually need a true systemic shock. The available evidence instead points to a still-bullish regime with ETF/institutional support and analysts focusing on downside levels around $95k-$80k, not $50k. I therefore put YES at 11%.

Mistral is a real contender, not a fringe pick: available pre-cutoff evidence says Mistral Medium 3.1 was 2nd overall on LM Arena as of late August and #1 in one important subcategory, which shows Mistral had a model within striking distance of the frontier. But the best evidence I found for the broader late-2025 state still points to Google/Gemini leading LMArena, and I did not find strong authoritative evidence by Nov. 9 that Mistral had already taken the top overall spot or had an imminent year-end launch likely to do so. With only ~7 weeks from forecast date to resolution and several heavyweight rivals still in the race, Mistral’s path to #1 looks plausible but clearly sub-50. I put it in the low-to-mid teens.

Racing Bulls were still only 6th on 72 points in the Sao Paulo week, and contemporaneous official F1 coverage framed Williams as having a 'firm grip' on 5th while the genuinely tight battle was Racing Bulls vs Aston Martin/Haas/Sauber for 6th-9th. With only three races and one sprint left after the cutoff, Racing Bulls likely needed a meaningful Williams slump plus a strong late run of their own. That is possible, and the tie-break favors Racing Bulls over Williams if they finish level, but the balance of evidence says 5th was an outside shot rather than a live favorite.

Kristen Foxen is an elite, accomplished player, but the event appears to be a 64-player single-elimination bracket, which makes any one entrant a long shot. She also drew Jesse Lonis, one of the strongest possible first-round opponents. I found no authoritative pre-Nov. 9 report explicitly saying Foxen had already won or even made a deep run, only that the already-played event had some leaked winner information circulating. Absent a specific spoiler naming her, the best estimate stays near the floor at 5%.

Zootopia 2 is a real contender, but the calendar-gross rules make this a very hard ask: it opens on Nov. 26, so only 36 days of domestic gross count (search_0_summary). Available reporting still had A Minecraft Movie as the 2025 domestic leader at about $423.9M, so Zootopia 2 likely needs a low-$400Ms run by Dec. 31 to win (search_3_result_1). Trade tracking for Zootopia 2 is strong at roughly $125M+ to $140M-$160M over the five-day Thanksgiving frame, but that looks more like 'plausible breakout' than 'record-smashing lock,' especially with Wicked: For Good and later Avatar competition in the same corridor (search_2_summary, search_0_summary). My view is that Zootopia 2 needs exceptional holiday legs and near-best-case word of mouth to get there, which is possible but materially less likely than not.

By the Nov. 9 cutoff, the evidence points to only five Starship test flights in all of 2025 through Flight 11 on Oct. 13, with major outlets describing Flight 11 as the final test flight of 2025 or the last flight before the next-generation vehicle begins flying later (search_1_result_2, search_5_result_6). Even granting the most generous interpretation that all five 2025 flights reached 62 miles, SpaceX would still need 5-7 additional successful Starship launches in the final weeks of the year to land in the 10-12 band, and there is no credible evidence of that kind of schedule; if anything, regulatory delays and lack of set launch dates point the other way (search_0_result_0, search_1_result_5, search_2_result_8).

Indiana looked like a clear long shot as of 2025-11-09. The strongest quantified signal is preseason division pricing that had Cleveland as an overwhelming favorite and Indiana around +3500, while the Pacers had also lost Tyrese Haliburton for the season and Myles Turner to Milwaukee. I found no strong evidence by the cutoff that early-season results had materially reversed that outlook; if anything, Indiana appeared to have a rough/injury-hit opening stretch. That leaves the Pacers with only a modest upset chance.

A U.S. invasion of Iran in the market’s sense requires more than strikes or brief raids; it requires commencing an offensive intended to establish control over Iranian territory. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the strongest evidence is that the June U.S. action was a limited airstrike episode, after which U.S. officials said no further operations were planned absent provocation, a ceasefire was announced, and later in 2025 the Pentagon was scaling back its Iraq mission rather than visibly massing forces for a ground campaign. Direct U.S.-Iran conflict clearly became possible in 2025, but the specific step from strikes to territorial invasion remains extremely unlikely with less than two months left in the year.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the strongest credible reporting describes a 2025 U.S. air/naval campaign against the Houthis, followed by a May ceasefire, with no direct credible report of regular U.S. troops physically entering Yemeni land territory. The main pro-YES evidence is that U.S. officials were open to supporting a local Yemeni ground operation, but that is materially weaker than evidence of actual U.S. regular-force entry, and the resolution excludes SOF/advisors/diplomatic visits. With most of the resolution window already elapsed and only about seven weeks remaining, the event still can happen, but it looks clearly less likely than not.

Marius Gierse was confirmed in the 64-player field, but any one player in a six-match single-elimination heads-up bracket has limited baseline win equity. The decisive evidence by the Nov. 9 cutoff is that multiple independent pre-cutoff articles reported a leak pointing to Sam Soverel as the winner, with Soverel's market price reportedly surging above 90% and even near 99%. That makes Gierse very unlikely absent a wrong or manipulated leak, though not literally impossible before official broadcast confirmation.

Google looks like a narrow favorite, not a lock. The strongest evidence is that Gemini 2.5 Pro was reportedly #1 on the LM Arena text leaderboard in early November 2025, and the forecast horizon is only about seven weeks. That said, Google’s lead was very small over Anthropic, the top spot has changed hands multiple times in 2025, and there is still meaningful risk from late-year releases or leaderboard drift—especially from Anthropic and a promised xAI Grok 5 launch. Net: current leader plus short horizon pushes Google above 50%, but only modestly.

I put this at 5%. WHO pandemic declarations are extremely rare, and by 9 Nov 2025 there was still no sign of any official WHO announcement declaring a new pandemic. The strongest 2025 candidate, mpox, was actually de-escalated: WHO reported that on 5 Sept 2025 the mpox upsurge no longer constituted a PHEIC (search_3_result_0). The main residual tail risk was H5N1, but WHO-linked late-2025 materials were still surveillance-oriented, and a joint FAO/WHO/WOAH assessment on WHO’s site assessed global public-health risk as low (search_4_result_7). With only ~7 weeks left in the year, YES would likely require a very sudden new respiratory event plus WHO choosing the formal word ‘pandemic’, which seemed unlikely.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, Merz was still serving as chancellor, with no credible reporting found of a resignation announcement, health crisis, or active removal process. The main pro-YES case is genuine coalition fragility: he started with a historically weak investiture and later reporting described the CDU/CSU-SPD coalition as split and hard to control. But Germany’s constructive no-confidence rules, the absence of an obvious replacement coalition, and the short remaining window to Dec. 31 make an actual exit or announced departure before year-end unlikely. I put the risk in the high single digits rather than near zero because intra-coalition shocks can happen, but NO remains the clear favorite.

Iran clearly retained dangerous breakout capability in 2025: the IAEA reported a very large 60%-enriched stockpile and past weapon-relevant work, so a bomb cannot be ruled out. But as of the Nov. 9 cutoff, the strongest recent evidence points against confirmed possession by year-end: U.S. intelligence still assessed Iran had not decided to build a bomb, Grossi said the IAEA saw no 'substantive work,' and much of the stockpile was believed trapped at bombed sites. Given the short remaining horizon and the market’s requirement for credible confirmation rather than mere capability, YES looks like a mid-teens outcome.

Meta looks materially behind with under two months to go. Multiple late-2025 snapshots indicate Meta is not in the top few Arena models, while rivals from Google/OpenAI/Anthropic/xAI are clustered near the top (search_1_result_9, search_3_summary). Meta’s main catch-up path, Behemoth, was delayed and still only in limited preview as of late September, making it less likely to accumulate enough public Arena voting in time (search_2_summary). There is still some upside because Meta has a history of aggressively testing many variants for Arena-style performance and reports suggested a Llama 4.X push for year-end release (search_0_summary, search_4_result_0), but the timing is tight and the leaderboard tie-break also slightly hurts Meta against Google/Anthropic. Net: a real but clearly minority chance.

By Nov. 9, 2025 Washington looked like a near-dead longshot. They were 3-6 and 13th in the NFC with a four-game losing streak and several lopsided recent losses (search_1_result_1, search_1_result_9, search_4_summary). Contemporaneous reporting put their playoff odds at just 4% and their chances to win or even reach Super Bowl LX at under 1% (search_4_summary, search_2_summary). The injury situation was also severe: Jayden Daniels had a dislocated elbow and was expected to miss several games, while the roster had 11 players on IR and Terry McLaurin was sidelined (search_3_summary, search_1_result_7). The main offset is that Daniels avoided surgery and was not placed on IR, so a later return remained possible (search_3_summary). My unconstrained estimate would be below 5%, but given the required probability floor, I submit 5%.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, The Life of a Showgirl had already banked 5 straight Billboard 200 weeks at #1, still posting 120,000 units in week 5 after a record 4.002 million-unit debut. That makes a 14-week run much more plausible than the generic base rate, especially given Taylor Swift’s very recent precedent with The Tortured Poets Department reaching 15 weeks at #1. Still, 9 more uninterrupted weeks are needed through mid-January, and a single major late-year release or holiday-season surge could break the streak, so I keep the probability clearly below 50%.

As of 2025-11-09, Bitcoin had already shown the ability to reach a fresh ATH around $125.8k in early October, so a $140k wick by year-end was clearly plausible. But the more decision-relevant evidence is that the rally appeared to have cooled materially by late October/early November: Reuters reported a sharp post-ATH drawdown to roughly $110k-$113k, there was a historically large liquidation event, outside snippets placed BTC around $110k entering November, and a Nov. 6 institutional note summarized by search results cut a year-end target to $120k amid ETF selling. From roughly $110k-$115k, hitting $140k before Dec. 31 would require about a 22%-27% rally in under two months. Bitcoin can absolutely do that, and only an intraminute high is needed, but given the weakening momentum and softer flows by the cutoff, I put the chance at about one in three.

As of 2025-11-09, Burden looks like a very long shot. The strongest evidence is his own production: through Week 8/into Week 9 he had just 13 catches, 171 yards, and 1 TD, with recent limited output and time in concussion protocol (search_1_summary). That is well behind a typical OROY pace. Meanwhile, multiple midseason OROY/rankings pieces highlighted other rookies—Jeanty, Ward, Egbuka, Tyler Warren, Jaxson Dart—while Burden was absent from the serious-contender discussion (search_0_summary; search_3_summary; search_5_result_1; search_5_result_8). With only one award available and several better-positioned rookies already ahead, Burden’s path requires both a huge second-half surge and stumbles from the leaders. That can happen, but it’s unlikely enough that I stay at the practical floor.

Sentimental Value looks like a serious Best Picture contender as of 2025-11-09: it has elite critical reception, Cannes Grand Prix pedigree, Trier’s prior Academy goodwill, and a strong Neon campaign with an Oscar-friendly Nov. 7 release (search_0_summary, search_2_summary, search_4_summary). But the best evidence suggests it is more in the contender pack than the clear leader: Gold Derby had it only No. 6 in September, it faces internal Neon competition from other international titles, and some late-2025 chatter treats films like Hamnet and Sinners as stronger frontrunners (search_4_summary, search_2_summary, search_5_result_1, search_5_result_2). That makes nomination likely, but winning still a low-probability outcome relative to the field.

As of 2025-11-09, this looks like a close but sub-50% shot. The bar to clear is high: A Minecraft Movie already posted about a $163M domestic opening, which major trades described as the biggest domestic debut of 2025. Wicked: For Good has very strong signals — record 2025 first-day Fandango presales, presales ahead of the first film, and some bullish forecasts reaching the mid/high $100Ms. But the more authoritative mainstream trade ranges I found were still mostly around $112M-$120M, with Boxoffice Pro at $130M-$155M. Since other late-2025 challengers like Zootopia 2 and Avatar: Fire and Ash appear to be tracking lower on a 3-day basis, the question mostly reduces to whether Wicked can materially overperform and beat Minecraft’s ~$163M benchmark. I think it has a real chance, but not the likeliest outcome.

I land at 5%. As of Nov. 9, 2025, the film appears to be a fringe contender rather than a real Best Production Design frontrunner. The biggest issue is the nomination hurdle: recent awards analysis places Springsteen: Deliver Me from Nowhere outside the main predicted nominees in this category, while broader Oscar momentum for the film weakened after release. This category also tends to favor more overtly showy design achievements, and rivals like Wicked: For Good and Frankenstein are the repeatedly named heavyweights. There is still a small path if the film’s period recreation gains late craft support, but based on the available pre-cutoff evidence, YES looks unlikely.

Base rate for a named player in a 64-player single-elimination heads-up championship is about 1.6%. Ryan Feldman was in the field, but he drew Sean Winter in round one, a very unfavorable matchup for a likely below-average entrant in this field. The biggest late evidence is October spoiler reporting that one player's win probability had gone to nearly 100% in informed market trading, with only Brian Rast, Erik Seidel, and Sean Winter still above 1% besides the presumed winner; that strongly suggests Feldman was already an extreme long shot by Nov. 9. I cannot verify the leaked winner's name directly from available pre-cutoff sources, so I won't go lower than the platform/developer floor, but my true estimate would be near the floor.

DeepSeek looks like a genuine top-tier contender, but the best pre-cutoff evidence points to it being competitive rather than the standing overall #1 on Chatbot Arena. Reporting indicates Gemini 2.5 Pro held the top spot earlier in 2025, GPT-5 was reported #1 across categories in August, and Grok/Claude/OpenAI/Google all remained strong rivals. DeepSeek’s strongest documented position is top open-source / upper-echelon overall, plus continued iteration with V3.1, but I found no solid pre-Nov-9 evidence that a DeepSeek model had taken or was clearly about to take the overall Arena lead. With only ~7 weeks from cutoff to resolution, current position matters a lot. I therefore put DeepSeek in the low-teens: live enough to win via a strong late release or leaderboard churn, but still behind the field of incumbent leaders.

OpenAI looks like a serious but not dominant favorite. The strongest pro-OpenAI evidence is that it already reached #1 on Arena in 2025 and then launched GPT-5 in August 2025, which outside reporting treated as one of the leading frontier models. Over only ~7 weeks from the Nov. 9 cutoff to Dec. 31, incumbency/near-incumbency matters. But several factors keep this below 50%: Arena leadership is highly volatile, multiple rival labs are credible contenders, OpenAI loses alphabetic tiebreaks to Anthropic/Google/DeepSeek, and xAI had publicly signaled a Grok 5 launch before year-end. The biggest missing piece is the exact Nov. 9 leaderboard state; without that, I lean only slightly toward OpenAI being the single most likely lab, but not more likely than all competitors combined.

By the Nov. 9 cutoff, essentially all authoritative U.S. signals pointed the same way: 3I/ATLAS was being treated as a natural interstellar comet, not as alien life or technology. NASA had classified it as a comet and publicly pushed back on alien-spacecraft claims (search_0_result_0, search_1_result_4, search_1_result_8). NASA-linked observations also reported ordinary cometary features like dust and a CO2-rich coma (search_2_result_0, search_2_result_3). Most importantly for this market, by Nov. 4 the Pentagon/AARO reportedly assessed it as a comet, not a UAP, and referred questions to NASA (search_3_result_0). A speculative Avi Loeb paper created publicity, but not official uptake (search_1_result_1). Given the extreme base-rate rarity of definitive U.S. government alien confirmations, and the fact that late-breaking official assessments were still firmly naturalistic, the true probability is probably below 5%; I submit the platform floor of 5%.

By Nov. 9, 2025, the Fed had already made two 25bp cuts (September and October), leaving only the Dec. 9-10 meeting before resolution. So YES effectively required another 50bp-equivalent of easing in a month, most plausibly a 50bp December cut or the very unlikely combination of an intermeeting 25bp cut plus a December 25bp cut. The evidence points to December 25bp as the central case, not 50bp: Powell said a December cut was not assured, Reuters/LSEG pricing after the October meeting pointed to roughly a 67.9% chance of a 25bp cut, and Reuters polling showed only a tiny minority of economists expecting a 50bp December move. A 50bp move was not impossible—one governor dissented for 50bp in October, and some analysts flagged downside labor-market risk—but it looked like a tail scenario rather than the modal outcome.

By the Nov. 9 cutoff, Portland looked effectively dead. The strongest evidence is an official NBA.com page published Nov. 5 listing the eight 2025 NBA Cup knockout-round teams — Heat, Knicks, Lakers, Magic, Raptors, Spurs, Suns and Thunder — and Portland is not among them, implying the Blazers had already failed to advance and were eliminated before the cutoff. Independent context also points the same way: Portland was only 2-2 in group play, was in a widely described 'group of death,' and entered as a longshot even to win its group. If the knockout field was already set without Portland, then the true probability is essentially 0; I’m submitting the 5% floor required by the tool/instructions.

I estimate a 5% chance of YES. By Nov. 9, 2025, the most plausible candidate allies—Japan, South Korea, and Poland—were still in the realm of debate, deterrence policy, or nuclear-sharing/hosting proposals, not independent possession of an operational weapon. Reuters reporting showed Japan was only reassessing options, South Korea said it would not pursue nuclear weapons, and Poland’s proposal concerned hosting U.S. warheads under nuclear sharing, which the market explicitly excludes. Given the extreme historical rarity of a US ally newly acquiring independent nukes, the short time remaining in 2025, and the absence of clear signs like NPT withdrawal, breakout activity, testing, or official confirmation, NO is overwhelmingly more likely. I leave a small residual for a surprise covert possession being officially confirmed before year-end.

I estimate a 12% chance of a qualifying NATO-Russia military encounter by March 31, 2026. The main reason it is not lower is that the operational tempo around NATO’s eastern flank was clearly elevated in late 2025: Russian drones entered Poland in September, Russian jets violated Baltic NATO airspace, and NATO commanders said Moscow would keep testing boundaries (search_0_summary, search_1_summary). That creates a real tail risk of a shootdown or exchange of fire over the next ~5 months.

But the strongest evidence points against a qualifying clash. First, the most serious recent use-of-force incident was Poland’s Sept. 9 drone shootdown, which happened before this market’s Oct. 8 start date (search_0_summary). Second, many of the incidents since then are exactly the kinds of events the rules tend to exclude: airspace violations followed by escorts/intercepts, hybrid pressure, or likely one-way attack drones aimed at Ukraine rather than NATO (search_0_summary, search_1_summary, search_4_summary). Third, Reuters reported that by late October NATO believed Russia had been somewhat deterred and was being more careful, while continuing mainly with hybrid methods rather than direct military confrontation (search_1_summary). The late-October Polish interception over the Baltic also appears to have remained below the threshold of weapons use (search_4_summary).

So: risk is materially above peacetime, but still clearly below even odds because both sides retain strong incentives to avoid direct combat and many plausible incidents would not satisfy this market’s narrow resolution criteria.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the balance of evidence strongly favors YES. Multiple independent tech outlets reported that Apple introduced an updated Vision Pro on Oct. 15, 2025, opened pre-orders immediately, and began customer deliveries/in-store sales on Oct. 22, 2025. That satisfies the key release criterion of public availability before Dec. 31, 2025. The remaining ambiguity is definitional: the product appears to be a same-name M5 refresh rather than a dramatically redesigned 'Vision Pro 2.' But the market rules explicitly allow a successor that is not numbered, and several reports described it as a second-generation Vision Pro. Apple's own support documentation distinguishing a 2025 Vision Pro model from the 2024 original further reduces the risk that this was merely a trivial configuration update. So I assign a high probability of YES, with only modest residual risk from interpretation of 'successor.'

As of 2025-11-09, the Eagles look like one of the NFL’s best Super Bowl contenders but not a dominant favorite. They were 6-2 entering Week 10, were coming off a bye and two strong wins, and Jalen Hurts was healthy with A.J. Brown returning (search_5_summary, search_4_summary). Multiple contemporaneous previews described them as either the Super Bowl 60 favorite or one of the top two title probabilities in the league (search_5_result_0, search_5_result_9). That said, the broader market was crowded with several near-peers, and one late-October odds snapshot still had Philadelphia at +1000, which is strong but far from overwhelming (search_2_result_3). Netting this out, a probability in the high teens is appropriate.

I estimate a 5% chance of YES. The strongest pro-YES evidence is that credible media in May 2025 amplified Michael Wolff's claim that the Trumps are effectively separated and that Melania was spending very little time at the White House, which suggests a genuinely strained or unconventional marriage. But for this market, the key event is not marital strain in general; it is a public announcement of intent to divorce by year-end 2025. Against that, we have an official White House denial, Melania's own January 2025 statements describing herself as Trump's wife and treating disagreements as normal, repeated debunking of fake filing rumors, and—most importantly—no announcement at all by November 9, leaving only about seven weeks. Given the very low base rate for a sitting president/first lady publicly announcing divorce during the first year of a term, NO remains overwhelmingly more likely.

I estimate a 7% chance. Musk did already hold a formal Trump-administration role in early 2025, so a return is procedurally possible, but the dominant evidence points the other way: his May exit was planned and real, SGEs face time limits, and the Trump-Musk relationship then blew up publicly in June with threats over contracts and impeachment talk. After several targeted searches, I found no credible fall-2025 reporting or official White House announcement indicating a formal reappointment was in motion, and by Nov. 9 only a short year-end window remained.

I estimate about a 7% chance that Stephen Chidwick is the eventual official winner. The event is a completed 64-player single-elimination heads-up bracket, so the random baseline is only 1.6%. Chidwick is confirmed in the field and is one of the strongest players in it, which lifts him well above average, and his early draw looked relatively manageable. But six consecutive heads-up wins are still a very tall order, especially with multiple other elite players in his section. Pre-cutoff reporting also indicates the event was taped in August and the result may have leaked by October, but the searches here did not credibly identify Chidwick as the leaked winner, so I don’t move him sharply up or down from a strong-player prior.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the outcome appears very likely to have already occurred. Major outlets had already reported Israeli airstrikes on Iranian soil in Oct. 2024, and Reuters reporting from June 13-15, 2025 is even clearer: Israel conducted major strikes on Iranian territory including Natanz, Tehran-area targets, missile factories, and other sites. Those are Israeli military air/missile strikes impacting Iranian ground territory, which matches the market definition. The only reason not to go to certainty is residual market-resolution ambiguity, but on the facts this looks effectively already resolved YES.

By 2025-11-09, the weight of authoritative evidence pointed to 2025 finishing extremely warm but short of 2024. Copernicus said on Nov. 6 that 2025 was virtually certain to rank second or third warmest, not first (search_1_result_0). WMO likewise said on Nov. 7 that 2025 was set to be second or third warmest and that Jan–Aug 2025 was lower than 2024 (search_4_result_8). Earlier in the year, Carbon Brief and NOAA-based analyses repeatedly said a new record was unlikely or very unlikely, with NOAA giving only a 4% chance in February and Carbon Brief saying in July that 2025 was on track for second or third warmest (search_3_result_7, search_2_result_1). Since the resolution source is NASA GISTEMP rather than Copernicus/NOAA, I leave some tail risk for dataset differences and a very hot Nov–Dec, but the consensus trajectory strongly favors NO.

Best Makeup and Hairstyling usually rewards films with obvious prosthetics, creature/fantasy looks, horror transformations, or highly conspicuous period styling. Highest 2 Lowest appears instead to be a contemporary crime drama, and the available coverage I found points to general/acting buzz rather than makeup-specific strength. I found no sign that it was an expert-listed contender in this category, and its limited theatrical push further weakens an already narrow path. So its chance is basically at the low end of plausible outcomes: it would need a surprise nomination first, then an even bigger upset win.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, gold was already far above the $3,200 threshold and had been for months. Reuters reported U.S. gold futures settled at $3,244.6 on Apr. 11, showing the threshold had already been cleared well before year-end (search_1_result_7). More importantly, late-Oct/early-Nov Reuters and World Gold Council coverage indicated gold had recently peaked at $4,381, was trading in a range from the high $3,000s to mid-$4,000s, and had first key technical support around $3,800 as of Nov. 5 (search_2_result_4, search_2_result_6, search_2_result_2). That leaves a large cushion above $3,200 with less than two months to go. The main risk is a sharp macro reversal—stronger dollar, fewer Fed cuts, geopolitical de-escalation, or profit-taking—since gold had already shown it could correct more than 8% from peak late in October (search_2_result_4). But requiring a roughly 15-25% further drop from early-Nov levels to finish below $3,200 seems materially less likely than not. So YES is very likely, though not certain.

Z.ai/Zhipu looks like a real contender, not a fringe name: Z.ai is tied to the GLM-4.5 family, Zhipu had an active 2025 release cadence through GLM-4.6, and GLM 4.6 showed notable strength on Arena sub-leaderboards. But the key issue is the overall leaderboard that determines resolution. The best near-cutoff evidence I found has the overall top tightly contested among other models/labs, with Grok/LongCat/DeepSeek near the top and no indication that a Z.ai-owned model was already #1 or clearly within immediate striking distance. With only about seven weeks from the cutoff to resolution, Z.ai still has some upset path via a late model climb or release, but absent evidence of an imminent stronger launch or a current near-top overall rank, that path looks materially less likely than not.

AICO appears to be a small indigenous party, not a major national Senate contender. The strongest direct evidence is that in 2022 it won only 1 Senate seat, and that seat came from the special indigenous constituency rather than the main nationwide contest. Meanwhile, 2025 reporting on the 2026 election landscape focuses on much larger blocs such as Pacto Histórico and other nationwide coalitions, with no indication that AICO is emerging as the ballot label for a broad alliance. Given that winning the most seats would likely require a jump from roughly 1 seat to the national plurality, I put AICO at the minimum allowed probability.

I estimate a 39% chance. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, Bitcoin had clearly entered a risk-off, high-volatility phase: Bloomberg said it was below $107k after October’s historic liquidation event, CNBC reported it broke below $100k on Nov. 4, and summaries indicate the Nov. 4 low was about $98,892 before rebounding above $103k by Nov. 7. That leaves the $90k barrier only about 13% below the rebound level, which is very reachable in Bitcoin over a ~7-week window. But the strongest recent evidence against is that even the record October washout only got to roughly $104.8k, and the early-November break was bought fairly quickly, suggesting meaningful support above $90k. Net: a substantial but still sub-50% chance of a $90k-or-lower 1-minute Binance wick by Dec. 31, 2025.
